text: Carbohydrate supplementation during exercise produces statistically significant performance benefits in 82% of studies across varying exercise durations.
The performance benefit of carbohydrate intake increases with longer total exercise duration, showing a significant correlative relationship.

For short duration exercise (~1 hour), simply rinsing the mouth with carbohydrate solution enhances performance via central nervous system stimulation of pleasure and reward centers, without needing to absorb or oxidize the carbohydrate.
For short duration exercise (~1 hour), the type, amount, absorption rate, and oxidation rate of carbohydrate are irrelevant to performance enhancement — only oral receptor exposure matters.
For prolonged exercise (>2 hours), high rates of carbohydrate delivery exceeding 90 g/h are needed to enhance performance through high rates of carbohydrate oxidation.

Multiple transportable carbohydrates using glucose:fructose combinations are beneficial for prolonged exercise because they enable higher total carbohydrate absorption via separate intestinal transporters.
Carbohydrate fueling recommendations for prolonged exercise should be individualized according to each athlete's gastrointestinal tolerance rather than applying a universal protocol.
